72cdbc99-2707-4817-ac40-435a03e5e837.advice:
It is possible that you have overlooked existing data! This question is not only about data sets that are similar to what you want to determine yourself, but also reference data or data that should be mined from the existing literature. Further, it is very likely that you will refer to related data, e.g. other databases where you usually "quickly look something up", but that could maybe be properly integrated, especially if you need to do such lookups multiple times.

17d33b84-bb8b-4222-8aa3-abb86f86a89d.text:
This chapter addresses what will happen with the data (at latest) when the project ends. Deletion of data, data publication and long term archiving are addressed.

[Supporting information for this chapter](https://plan.research-data.no/pages/support_08_preserve_publish)
17d33b84-bb8b-4222-8aa3-abb86f86a89d.title: Archiving and publishing data

1e85da40-bbfc-4180-903e-6c569ed2da38.text:
Remember to select the right project phase before filling out/updating the DMP: [User guidance](https://plan.research-data.no/pages/support_00_start_writing)

This chapter collects administrative information about the DMP itself.

[Supporting information for this chapter](https://plan.research-data.no/pages/support_01_about_dmp)
1e85da40-bbfc-4180-903e-6c569ed2da38.title: About the Data Management Plan

45ca259a-aa6e-4539-b4bb-5a3036054902.text:
This chapter collects information about legal and ethical aspects which may have implications on research data management in the project.

[Supporting information for this chapter](https://plan.research-data.no/pages/support_03_legal_ethics)
45ca259a-aa6e-4539-b4bb-5a3036054902.title: Legal and ethical aspects

95c9e49e-c059-4630-b8dc-6a6bdd25a864.text:
This chapters collects administrative information about the research project this DMP is written for.

[Supporting information for this chapter](https://plan.research-data.no/pages/support_02_about_research)
95c9e49e-c059-4630-b8dc-6a6bdd25a864.title: About the research project

9c8b4bfc-14e1-4a9b-930a-7608aa9c579f.text:
Regardless of the origin of the data in your project, data will likely be further processed, analysed and interpreted.

[Supporting information for this chapter](https://plan.research-data.no/pages/support_07_process_analyse)
9c8b4bfc-14e1-4a9b-930a-7608aa9c579f.title: Processing, analysing and interpreting data

a917a6bd-27d9-4014-ab62-85adf968bddb.text:
This chapter collects information about the type and origin of the data that will be included in the project. This could be both datasets and records originating outside the project, as well as novel data collected, captured, generated or created by you or other project members. Different disciplines may use different terms to describe the data or records underlying a research project.

In this chapter, an inclusive approach is used with 'data' functioning as a broad and overarching term. This includes observational and experimental data, surveys, registry data, simulations, as well as data in digital museum archives and other data records.

[Supporting information for this chapter](https://plan.research-data.no/pages/support_05_reuse_collect)
a917a6bd-27d9-4014-ab62-85adf968bddb.title: Reusing and collecting data

b1df3c74-0b1f-4574-81c4-4cc2d780c1af.text:
This chapter collects information about how metadata and other accompanying information will be handled in the active phase of the project. The planned use of metadata will benefit the research project both internally, among project members, as well as help make the data understandable and FAIR for external users and future reuse.  

[Supporting information for this chapter](https://plan.research-data.no/pages/support_06_document)
b1df3c74-0b1f-4574-81c4-4cc2d780c1af.title: Data documentation during the project

d1622fbf-e765-4eb0-aba3-dcaa6fe50b07.text:
This chapters collects information about data storage and backup throughout the research project. Consider all digital data and documents that you are expecting to handle in the course of the project, including working documents.

If specific solutions are chosen during data processing and analysis or preservation, this may be specified in the respective chapters.

[Supporting information for this chapter](https://plan.research-data.no/pages/support_04_store_protect)
d1622fbf-e765-4eb0-aba3-dcaa6fe50b07.title: Storing and protecting data during the project

d970ba78-e9eb-4486-90af-b0ba50aec2f0.text:
Clearly define responsibilities for research data management in the project and identify any associated costs. Use this section to document roles, resource needs, and expected expenses.

[Supporting information for this chapter](https://plan.research-data.no/pages/support_09_responsibilities_resources)
d970ba78-e9eb-4486-90af-b0ba50aec2f0.title: Responsibilities and resources

International","rightsUri":"https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/legalcode","schemeUri":"https://spdx.org/licenses/","rightsIdentifier":"cc-by-4.0","rightsIdentifierScheme":"SPDX"}],"descriptions":[{"description":"Research Data Alliance (RDA) ble lansert i 2013 som en medlemsdrevet organisasjon, med en overordnet målsetting om å tilrettelegge for åpen deling av forskningsdata på tvers av teknologier, fagfelt, og land. RDA Norge er den regionale gruppen for Norge, og har 235 medlemmer. Arbeidsgruppen for formidling og kompetansebygging er en fast gruppe under RDA Norge og består av medlemmer fra de institusjonelle medlemmene i RDA-Norge. 

 

Gruppens hovedformål er å bidra til nasjonal koordinering og økt kunnskap om ulike tema innen forskningsdatahåndtering, da dette er et felt i vekst der det skjer mye både nasjonalt og internasjonalt. Målet for gruppen er å gjøre det lettere for kolleger i det norske praksisfeltet å holde seg oppdatert. Dette gjøres gjennom webinarserier og andre arrangementer eksempelvis bidrag til OA Week og erfaringsutveksling under konferanser som Research Data Alliance Plenaries. Høsten 2024 har vi hatt med oss representanter fra nasjonale og internasjonale infrastrukturaktører som har snakket om European Open Science Cloud (EOSC); Hvordan involvere seg i RDA; metadata for forskningsdata og Global open research commons (GORC) modellen. Presentasjoner holdt på arrangementene våre samles i et dedikert Zenodo community.  I vår presentasjon vil vi fortelle mer om pågående og kommende aktiviteter og hvordan gruppen er organisert.

International","rightsUri":"https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/legalcode","schemeUri":"https://spdx.org/licenses/","rightsIdentifier":"cc-by-4.0","rightsIdentifierScheme":"SPDX"}],"descriptions":[{"description":"Research Data Alliance (RDA) ble lansert i 2013 som en medlemsdrevet organisasjon, med en overordnet målsetting om å tilrettelegge for åpen deling av forskningsdata på tvers av teknologier, fagfelt, og land. RDA Norge er den regionale gruppen for Norge, og har 235 medlemmer. Arbeidsgruppen for formidling og kompetansebygging er en fast gruppe under RDA Norge og består av medlemmer fra de institusjonelle medlemmene i RDA-Norge. 

 

Gruppens hovedformål er å bidra til nasjonal koordinering og økt kunnskap om ulike tema innen forskningsdatahåndtering, da dette er et felt i vekst der det skjer mye både nasjonalt og internasjonalt. Målet for gruppen er å gjøre det lettere for kolleger i det norske praksisfeltet å holde seg oppdatert. Dette gjøres gjennom webinarserier og andre arrangementer eksempelvis bidrag til OA Week og erfaringsutveksling under konferanser som Research Data Alliance Plenaries. Høsten 2024 har vi hatt med oss representanter fra nasjonale og internasjonale infrastrukturaktører som har snakket om European Open Science Cloud (EOSC); Hvordan involvere seg i RDA; metadata for forskningsdata og Global open research commons (GORC) modellen. Presentasjoner holdt på arrangementene våre samles i et dedikert Zenodo community.  I vår presentasjon vil vi fortelle mer om pågående og kommende aktiviteter og hvordan gruppen er organisert.

Presentasjon på 9. seminar for Bibliotekenes nettverk for ph.d.-støtte 24.-25.04.2025.","descriptionType":"Abstract"},{"lang":"nor","description":"Datahåndteringsplaner (DHP) har blitt et etablert verktøy i forskningsprosessen og dermed også ph.d.-utdanningen. En DHP er et formelt dokument som beskriver hvordan data skal håndteres gjennom et forskningsprosjekt, fra innsamling eller gjenbruk av data til arkivering, og i henhold til gjeldende regelverk og etiske vurderinger. God planlegging og etablering av gode praksis er en forutsetning for å kunne publisere FAIR forskningsdata. Å skrive en DHP er derfor en bevisstgjørende øvelse, men en DHP kan i tillegg være et praktisk verktøy som bidrar til effektiv informasjonsflyt i forskningsprosessen. God veiledning er avgjørende for at skriving av en DHP oppleves som en nyttig prosess med relevans for forskningsprosjektet.UiB, UiO, NTNU, UiT («BOTT-universitetene») begynte å samarbeide om datahåndteringsplan-veiledning i 2022. Kunnskapsressursen plan.research-data.no ble lansert i november 2024 som resultat av prosjektet «Datahåndteringsplaner: støttepakke for norske UH-bibliotek». Prosjektet var et samarbeid mellom UiB, UiO, NTNU, UiT, Elixir Norge og PhD-On-Track og mottok støtte fra Nasjonalbiblioteket i 2023. Prosjektresultatene omfatter en modulær DHP-mal tilpasset norske forhold, tilknyttet støtteinformasjon og generell informasjon om DHP-er, samt ressurser for forskningsstøtte. Ressursene er tilgjengelige for gjenbruk under CC-BY og vedlikeholdes av en redaksjonsgruppe gjennom en åpen bidragsprosess. Pilotering av DHP-verktøyet FAIR Wizard Norway i første halvdelen av 2025 i et BOTT-samarbeid i regi av NTNU ga anledning til bred testing av ressursene. Vi deler resultater fra både prosjektet og testperioden, samt erfaringer med å skape forskningsstøtteressurser gjennom en åpen samskrivingsprosess.","descriptionType":"Abstract"}],"geoLocations":[],"fundingReferences":[],"url":"https://zenodo.org/doi/10.5281/zenodo.15350121","contentUrl":null,"metadataVersion":1,"schemaVersion":"http://datacite.org/schema/kernel-4","source":"api","isActive":true,"state":"findable","reason":null,"viewCount":0,"downloadCount":0,"referenceCount":0,"citationCount":0,"partCount":0,"partOfCount":0,"versionCount":1,"versionOfCount":0,"created":"2025-05-06T12:56:49Z","registered":"2025-05-06T12:56:49Z","published":null,"updated":"2025-05-28T15:10:45Z"},"relationships":{"client":{"data":{"id":"cern.zenodo","type":"clients"}}}},{"id":"10.5281/zenodo.15350122","type":"dois","attributes":{"doi":"10.5281/zenodo.15350122","identifiers":[{"identifier":"oai:zenodo.org:15350122","identifierType":"oai"}],"creators":[{"name":"Ostrop, Jenny","nameType":"Personal","givenName":"Jenny","familyName":"Ostrop","affiliation":["University of Bergen"],"nameIdentifiers":[{"nameIdentifier":"0000-0003-2752-8377","nameIdentifierScheme":"ORCID"}]}],"titles":[{"title":"Støtte til planlegging av datahåndtering i forskningsbibliotek - Presentasjon på VIRAK - konferansen for universitets- og høgskolebibliotek 2025.","descriptionType":"Abstract"},{"lang":"nor","description":"Forskningsdatatjenester og støtte til skriving av datahåndteringsplaner utgjør en stadig større del av forskningsstøtte i UH-bibliotek. For å lette arbeidet til både forskere og støttepersonell har prosjektet ‘Datahåndteringsplaner: Støttepakke for norske UH-bibliotek’ utarbeidet helhetlige ressurser.

En disiplinuavhengig mal for datahåndteringsplan i form av et spørreskjema legger til rette for både etablering av gode datahåndteringsrutiner i forskningsprosessen og bevisstgjøring og utveksling av strukturert informasjon med interessenter. En modulær oppbygging tillater å tilpasse et omfattende spørreskjema til ulike brukergrupper ved forhåndsutvalg og betingede spørsmål. Malen er supplert med omfattende veiledningsressurser som er tilgjengelige på https://plan.research-data.no. Vi gir en omvisning i de forskerrettede ressursene og viser hvordan malen kan brukes i DHP-verktøyet Data Stewardship Wizard/FAIR Wizard og hvordan veiledningstekstene støtter opp om spørsmålene. Demonstrasjonen avsluttes med en oversikt over sidene med bakgrunnsinformasjon og kunnskapsgrunnlaget for støttepersonell, og et innblikk i hvordan de åpne ressursene eventuelt kan tilpasses.","descriptionType":"Abstract"}],"geoLocations":[],"fundingReferences":[],"url":"https://zenodo.org/doi/10.5281/zenodo.15535362","contentUrl":null,"metadataVersion":0,"schemaVersion":"http://datacite.org/schema/kernel-4","source":"api","isActive":true,"state":"findable","reason":null,"viewCount":0,"downloadCount":0,"referenceCount":0,"citationCount":0,"partCount":0,"partOfCount":0,"versionCount":0,"versionOfCount":1,"created":"2025-05-28T14:55:46Z","registered":"2025-05-28T14:55:47Z","published":null,"updated":"2025-05-28T14:55:48Z"},"relationships":{"client":{"data":{"id":"cern.zenodo","type":"clients"}}}},{"id":"10.5281/zenodo.15535361","type":"dois","attributes":{"doi":"10.5281/zenodo.15535361","identifiers":[],"creators":[{"name":"Jahren, Lisbeth","nameType":"Personal","givenName":"Lisbeth","familyName":"Jahren","affiliation":["Norwegian University of Science and Technology"],"nameIdentifiers":[{"nameIdentifier":"0009-0003-2308-3099","nameIdentifierScheme":"ORCID"}]},{"name":"Heggland, Ingrid","nameType":"Personal","givenName":"Ingrid","familyName":"Heggland","affiliation":["Norwegian University of Science and Technology"],"nameIdentifiers":[{"nameIdentifier":"0000-0001-8466-1014","nameIdentifierScheme":"ORCID"}]},{"name":"Ostrop, Jenny","nameType":"Personal","givenName":"Jenny","familyName":"Ostrop","affiliation":["University of Bergen"],"nameIdentifiers":[{"nameIdentifier":"0000-0003-2752-8377","nameIdentifierScheme":"ORCID"}]}],"titles":[{"title":"Skriv en datadhåndteringsplan: demo av plan.research-data.no - International","rightsUri":"https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/legalcode","schemeUri":"https://spdx.org/licenses/","rightsIdentifier":"cc-by-4.0","rightsIdentifierScheme":"SPDX"}],"descriptions":[{"description":"Academic library support services face a growing demand for data management planning support. Providing guidance is essential to help researchers establish good data management routines and to prevent them from feeling overwhelmed by demands or perceiving the writing of a DMP as a pure administrative burden. At the same time, it is challenging for support personnel to keep up to date with guidelines and guidance resources in a fast-paced field.

To address both demands, the 1-year project ‘Data Management Plans: Support package for Norwegian higher education libraries’ (October 2023-2024) facilitated alignment of Data Management Plan (DMP) recommendations between the four largest Universities in Norway and developed resources for both researchers and support staff.

The support package consists of three products: 1) a knowledge base for support staff including annotation of the Science Europe Practical Guide to the International Alignment of Research Data Management in the Norwegian context, 2) short texts for use in digital DMP tools that were implemented as discipline-agnostic knowledge model and connected questionnaires/templates in the DMP-tool Data Stewardship Wizard (DSW), 3) accompanying information resources for researchers and students. All generated text resources are maintained through an open contribution and maintenance d00c69be-f779-4468-abc7-a72b24cbe15d.name: FAIRsharing (database)

response.body: {"data":[{"id":1850,"registry":"database","record_type":"repository","name":"European Nucleotide Archive","abbreviation":"ENA","description":"The European Nucleotide Archive (ENA) is a globally comprehensive data resource for nucleotide sequence, spanning raw data, alignments and assemblies, functional and taxonomic annotation and rich contextual data relating to sequenced samples and experimental design. Serving both as the database of record for the output of the world's sequencing activity and as a platform for the management, sharing and publication of sequence data, the ENA provides a portfolio of services for submission, data management, search and retrieval across web and programmatic interfaces. The ENA is part of the International Nucleotide Sequence Database Collaboration (INSDC), which comprises the DNA DataBank of Japan (DDBJ), the European Molecular Biology Laboratory (EMBL), and GenBank at the NCBI. These three organizations exchange data on a daily basis.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.dj8nt8","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.dj8nt8","homepage":"http://www.ebi.ac.uk/ena","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":1865,"registry":"database","record_type":"repository","name":"The European Genome-phenome Archive","abbreviation":"EGA","description":"The European Genome-phenome Archive (EGA) is a service for permanent archiving and sharing of personally identifiable genetic, phenotypic, and clinical data generated for the purposes of biomedical research projects or in the context of research-focused healthcare systems. Access to data must be approved by the specified Data Access Committee (DAC).","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.mya1ff","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.mya1ff","homepage":"https://ega-archive.org/","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":3066,"registry":"database","record_type":"repository","name":"EBAS","abbreviation":null,"description":"EBAS is a database hosting observation data of atmospheric chemical composition and physical properties. EBAS hosts data submitted by data originators in support of a number of national and international programs ranging from monitoring activities to research projects. EBAS is developed and operated by the Norwegian Institute for Air Research (NILU).","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.b94a20","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.b94a20","homepage":"http://ebas.nilu.no/","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":1547,"registry":"database","record_type":"repository","name":"NCBI Genetic Sequence Database","abbreviation":"GenBank","description":"GenBank ® is the NIH genetic sequence database of annotated collections of all publicly available DNA sequences. GenBank is part of an International Nucleotide Sequence Database Collaboration, which comprises the DNA DataBank of Japan (DDBJ), the European Nucleotide Archive (ENA), and GenBank at NCBI. The GenBank database is designed to provide and encourage access within the scientific community to the most up-to-date and comprehensive DNA sequence information, and release available versioned collections every two months from the ftp site. As part of the International Nucleotide Sequence Database Collaboration (INSDC), a long-standing foundational initiative (operating between DDBJ, EMBL-EBI and NCBI), the INSDC covers the spectrum of data raw reads, through alignments and assemblies to functional annotation, enriched with contextual information relating to samples and experimental configurations. GenBank data submission types include:  mRNA sequences, Prokaryotic genes, Eukaryotic genes, rRNA and/or ITS, viral sequences, transposon or insertion sequences, microsatellite sequences, pseudogenes, cloning vectors, phylogenetic or population sets, and non-coding RNAs.\n","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.9kahy4","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.9kahy4","homepage":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/genbank/","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":1579,"registry":"database","record_type":"repository","name":"Encyclopedia of DNA Elements","abbreviation":"ENCODE","description":"The ENCODE (Encyclopedia of DNA Elements) Consortium is an international collaboration of research groups funded by the National Human Genome Research Institute (NHGRI). The goal of ENCODE is to build a comprehensive parts list of functional elements in the human genome including elements that act at the protein and RNA levels, and regulatory elements that control cells and circumstances in which a gene is active. ENCODE investigators employ a variety of assays and methods to identify functional elements. The discovery and annotation of gene elements is accomplished primarily by sequencing a diverse range of RNA sources, comparative genomics, integrative bioinformatic methods, and human curation. Regulatory elements are typically investigated through DNA hypersensitivity assays, assays of DNA methylation, and immunoprecipitation (IP) of proteins that interact with DNA and RNA, i.e., modified histones, transcription factors, chromatin regulators, and RNA-binding proteins, followed by sequencing.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.v0hbjs","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.v0hbjs","homepage":"https://www.encodeproject.org/","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":2860,"registry":"database","record_type":"knowledgebase","name":"DNA Modification Database","abbreviation":"DNAmod","description":"DNAmod is an open-source database (https://dnamod.hoffmanlab.org) that catalogues DNA modifications and provides a single source to learn about their properties. The database annotates the chemical properties and structures of all curated modified DNA bases, and a much larger list of candidate chemical entities. DNAmod includes manual annotations of available sequencing methods, descriptions of their occurrence in nature, and provides existing and suggested nomenclature. DNAmod enables researchers to rapidly review previous work, select mapping techniques, and track recent developments concerning modified bases of interest.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.qgMKai","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.qgMKai","homepage":"https://dnamod.hoffmanlab.org/","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":2220,"registry":"database","record_type":"knowledgebase","name":"ReMap","abbreviation":"ReMap","description":"ReMap is a large scale integrative analysis of DNA-binding experiments for Homo sapiens, Mus musculus, Drosophila melanogaster and Arabidopsis thaliana transcriptional regulators. The catalogues are the results of the manual curation of ChIP-seq, ChIP-exo, DAP-seq from public sources (GEO, ENCODE, ENA).  ReMap (https://remap.univ-amu.fr) aims to provide manually curated, high-quality catalogs of regulatory regions resulting from a large-scale integrative analysis of DNA-binding experiments in Human, Mouse, Fly and Arabidopsis thaliana for hundreds of transcription factors and regulators. In this 2022 update, we have uniformly processed >11 000 DNA-binding sequencing datasets from public sources across four species.  The four regulatory catalogs are browsable through track hubs at UCSC, Ensembl and NCBI genome browsers. Finally, ReMap 2022 comes with a new Cis Regulatory Module identification method, improved quality controls, faster search results, and better user experience with an interactive tour and video tutorials on browsing and filtering ReMap catalogs.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.c8dxsv","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.c8dxsv","homepage":"https://remap.univ-amu.fr/","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":2654,"registry":"database","record_type":"repository","name":"E-depot Dutch Archeology","abbreviation":"EDNA","description":"E-depot Dutch Archeology is a collection of acrcheological research reports and the associated data such as GIS data, photographs, field data and data tables.","doi":null,"url":"https://fairsharing.org/2654","homepage":"https://dans.knaw.nl/en/about/services/easy/edna","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":2131,"registry":"database","record_type":"knowledgebase_and_repository","name":"The mitochondrial DNA breakpoints database","abbreviation":"MitoBreak","description":"A comprehensive on-line resource with curated datasets of mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA) rearrangements.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.8fy3bn","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.8fy3bn","homepage":"http://mitobreak.portugene.com","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":2350,"registry":"database","record_type":"knowledgebase","name":"Database of local DNA conformers","abbreviation":"Dolce","description":"Dolce is a database of DNA structure motifs based on an automatic classification method consisting of the combination of supervised and unsupervised approaches. This workflow has been applied to analyze 816 X-ray and 664 NMR DNA structures released till February 2013. Dinucleotides with unassigned conformations are either classified into one of already known 24 classes or into one of six new classes among so far unclassifiable data, which were newly identified and annotated among X-ray structures by authors of this tool.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.jhjnp0","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.jhjnp0","homepage":"http://ich.vscht.cz/projects/dolce/viewHome","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":1572,"registry":"database","record_type":"knowledgebase","name":"DNA Data Bank of Japan","abbreviation":"DDBJ","description":"An annotated collection of all publicly available nucleotide and protein sequences. DDBJ collects sequence data mainly from Japanese researchers, as well as researchers in other countries. DDBJ is part of the International Nucleotide Sequence Database Collaboration (INSDC), which comprises the DNA DataBank of Japan (DDBJ), the European Molecular Biology Laboratory (EMBL), and GenBank at the NCBI. These three organizations exchange data on a daily basis.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.k337f0","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.k337f0","homepage":"https://www.ddbj.nig.ac.jp/ddbj","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":2823,"registry":"database","record_type":"knowledgebase","name":"DNA Methylation Interactive Visualization Database","abbreviation":"DNMIVD","description":"DNMIVD is a comprehensive annotation and interactive visualization database for DNA methylation profile of diverse human cancer constructed with high throughput microarray data from TCGA and GEO databases, and it also integrates some data from Pancan-meQTL and HACER databases.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.ZKahvn","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.ZKahvn","homepage":"http://www.unimd.org/dnmivd/","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":1624,"registry":"database","record_type":"knowledgebase","name":"The DNA Replication Origin Database","abbreviation":"OriDB","description":"This database summarizes our knowledge of replication origins in the budding yeast Saccharomyces cerevisiae. Each proposed origin site has been assigned a Status (Confirmed, Likely, or Dubious) expressing the confidence that the site genuinely corresponds to an origin.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.284kre","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.284kre","homepage":"http://cerevisiae.oridb.org/","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":2141,"registry":"database","record_type":"knowledgebase","name":"Plant DNA C-values database","abbreviation":"Plant C-Values","description":"A database containing genome size (C-value) data for all groups of land plants and red, green and brown algae.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.7qexb2","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.7qexb2","homepage":"https://cvalues.science.kew.org/","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":1822,"registry":"database","record_type":"knowledgebase","name":"The Database of Human DNA Methylation and Cancer","abbreviation":"MethyCancer","description":"The database of human DNA Methylation and Cancer (MethyCancer) is developed to study interplay of DNA methylation, gene expression and cancer. It hosts both highly integrated data of DNA methylation, cancer-related gene, mutation and cancer information from public resources, and the CpG Island (CGI) clones derived from our large-scale sequencing.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.mtg4ew","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.mtg4ew","homepage":"http://methycancer.psych.ac.cn/","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":5018,"registry":"database","record_type":"knowledgebase_and_repository","name":"MethMarkerDB: a comprehensive cancer DNA methylation biomarker database","abbreviation":"MethMarkerDB","description":"MethMarkerDB is a comprehensive cancer DNA methylation biomarker database based on WGBS data, providing precise genomic location information for biomarkers, including genes and DMRs. It offers various functions such as literature-based marker gene search, pan-cancer biomarker search, and diagnostic/prognostic tools, supporting the discovery and clinical application of novel DNA methylation biomarkers in cancer.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.9b469a","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.9b469a","homepage":"https://methmarkerdb.hzau.edu.cn/","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":1941,"registry":"database","record_type":"knowledgebase_and_repository","name":"Human Unidentified Gene-Encoded large proteins database","abbreviation":"HUGE","description":"HUGE is a database for human large proteins newly identified in the Kazusa cDNA project, the aim of which is to predict the primary structure of proteins from the sequences of human large cDNAs (>4 kb).","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.zx2ztd","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.zx2ztd","homepage":"http://www.kazusa.or.jp/huge/","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":1573,"registry":"database","record_type":"knowledgebase","name":"DNAtraffic","abbreviation":"DNAtraffic","description":"A database for systems biology of DNA dynamics during the cell life.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.rq71dw","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.rq71dw","homepage":"http://dnatraffic.ibb.waw.pl/","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":2104,"registry":"database","record_type":"knowledgebase","name":"RegulonDB","abbreviation":"RegulonDB","description":"RegulonDB is a model of the complex regulation of transcription initiation or regulatory network of the cell. On the other hand, it is also a model of the organization of the genes in transcription units, operons and simple and complex regulons. In this regard, RegulonDB is a computational model of mechanisms of transcriptional regulation.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.f0awr5","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.f0awr5","homepage":"https://regulondb.ccg.unam.mx/","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":2135,"registry":"database","record_type":"repository","name":"arrayMap - Genomic Array Data for Cancer CNV Profiles","abbreviation":"arrayMap","description":"Part of the Progenetix project, the arrayMap database facilitates the study of the genetics of human cancer. The Progenetix project provides the data customisation and visualization tools to mine the available data. The arrayMap database is developed by the group \"Theoretical Cytogenetics and Oncogenomics\" at the Department of Molecular Life Sciences of the University of Zurich.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.1fbc5y","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.1fbc5y","homepage":"http://arraymap.org","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":1943,"registry":"database","record_type":"knowledgebase_and_repository","name":"Yeast Searching for Transcriptional Regulators and Consensus Tracking","abbreviation":"YEASTRACT","description":"YEASTRACT (Yeast Search for Transcriptional Regulators And Consensus Tracking) is a curated repository of more than 48333 regulatory associations between transcription factors (TF) and target genes in Saccharomyces cerevisiae, based on more than 1200 bibliographic references.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.mphj4z","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.mphj4z","homepage":"http://www.yeastract.com","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":2228,"registry":"database","record_type":"knowledgebase","name":"WholeCellSimDB","abbreviation":"WholeCellSimDB","description":"WholeCellSimDB is a database of whole-cell model simulations designed to make it easy for researchers to explore and analyze whole-cell model predictions including predicted: - Metabolite concentrations, - DNA, RNA and protein expression, - DNA-bound protein positions, - DNA modification positions, and - Ribome positions.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.ew61fn","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.ew61fn","homepage":"http://www.wholecellsimdb.org","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":1595,"registry":"database","record_type":"knowledgebase","name":"HotRegion","abbreviation":"HotRegion","description":"Database of interaction Hotspots across the proteome. Hot spots are energetically important residues at protein interfaces and they are not randomly distributed across the interface but rather clustered. These clustered hot spots form hot regions. HotRegion, provides information of these interfaces by using predicted hot spot residues, and structural properties of these interface residues such as pair potentials of interface residues, accessible surface area (ASA) and relative ASA values of interface residues of both monomer and complex forms of proteins. Also, the 3D visualization of the interface and interactions among hot spot residues are provided.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.m4a6d3","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.m4a6d3","homepage":"http://prism.ccbb.ku.edu.tr/hotregion","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":1828,"registry":"database","record_type":"repository","name":"OryGenesDB: an interactive tool for rice reverse genetics","abbreviation":"OryGenesDB","description":"The aim of this Oryza sativa database was first to display sequence information such as the T-DNA and Ds flanking sequence tags (FSTs) produced in the framework of the French genomics initiative Genoplante and the EU consortium Cereal Gene Tags. This information was later linked with related molecular data from external rice molecular resources (cDNA full length, Gene, EST, Markers, Expression data...).","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.7m4hza","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.7m4hza","homepage":"http://orygenesdb.cirad.fr/","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":1649,"registry":"database","record_type":"knowledgebase_and_repository","name":"The Arabidopsis Information Resource","abbreviation":"TAIR","description":"The Arabidopsis Information Resource (TAIR) maintains a database of genetic and molecular biology data for the model higher plant Arabidopsis thaliana. Data available from TAIR includes the complete genome sequence along with gene structure, gene product information, gene expression, DNA and seed stocks, genome maps, genetic and physical markers, publications, and information about the Arabidopsis research community. Gene product function data is updated every week from the latest published research literature and community data submissions.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.4dqw0","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.4dqw0","homepage":"http://www.arabidopsis.org/","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":2064,"registry":"database","record_type":"knowledgebase","name":"Signaling Pathway Integrated Knowledge Engine","abbreviation":"SPIKE","description":"SPIKE (Signaling Pathway Integrated Knowledge Engine) is an interactive software environment that graphically displays biological signaling networks, allows dynamic layout and navigation through these networks, and enables the superposition of DNA microarray and other functional genomics data on interaction maps.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.scsnja","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.scsnja","homepage":"http://www.cs.tau.ac.il/~spike/","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":1798,"registry":"database","record_type":"repository","name":"Erythropoiesis Database","abbreviation":"EpoDB","description":"EpoDB (Erythropoiesis database) is a database of genes that relate to vertebrate red blood cells. It includes DNA sequence, structural features, protein information, gene expression information and transcription factor binding sites.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.krvr4","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.krvr4","homepage":"http://www.cbil.upenn.edu/EpoDB/","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":3719,"registry":"database","record_type":"repository","name":"MIRRI Information System Catalog","abbreviation":"MIRRI-IS","description":"The MIRRI Information System (MIRRI-IS) provides access to high-quality microbial resources made available by its 50+ partner biorepositories, covering all types of microorganisms, such as bacteria (and their cognate bacteriophages), archaea, fungi (including yeasts), eukaryotic viruses, micro-algae and other microbiological material such as cell lines, natural or constructs carrying plasmids, DNA libraries, and genomic DNA. It also provide users with all relevant information and associated contextual data (metadata) about the microbial resources, as available – e.g. taxonomy, ecology, pathogenicity, morphology, physiology, chemical characterization, DNA barcoding or genomics.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.926415","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.926415","homepage":"https://www.mirri.org/microbial-resources-data/","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":1805,"registry":"database","record_type":"knowledgebase","name":"Database of Rice Transcription Factors","abbreviation":"DRTF","description":"DRTF contains 2025 putative transcription factors (TFs) in Oryza sativa L. ssp. indica and 2384 in ssp. japonica, distributed in 63 families, identified by computational prediction and manual curation. It includes detailed annotations of each TF including sequence features, functional domains, Gene Ontology assignment, chromosomal localization, EST and microarray expression information, as well as multiple sequence alignment of the DNA-binding domains for each TF family.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.d8j0eb","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.d8j0eb","homepage":"http://drtf.cbi.pku.edu.cn/","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":1991,"registry":"database","record_type":"knowledgebase","name":"Reference Sequence Database","abbreviation":"RefSeq","description":"The Reference Sequence (RefSeq) collection aims to provide a comprehensive, integrated, non-redundant, well-annotated set of sequences, including genomic DNA, transcripts, and proteins.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.4jg0qw","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.4jg0qw","homepage":"http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/RefSeq/","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":4416,"registry":"database","record_type":"knowledgebase","name":"QUADRatlas","abbreviation":null,"description":"QUADRatlas is a database of experimentally-derived and computationally predicted RNA G-quadruplex (RG4s) in the human transcriptome, enriched with biological function and disease associations. QUADRatlas includes known interactions of RG4s with RNA-binding proteins (RBPs), complemented with an extensive RBP binding sites dataset. Users can thus intersect RG4s with their potential regulators and effectors, enabling the formulation of novel hypotheses on RG4 regulation, function and pathogenicity. To support this capability, we provide analysis tools for predicting whether an RBP can bind RG4s, RG4 enrichment in a gene set, and de novo RG4 prediction. Genome-browser and table views allow exploring, filtering, and downloading the data quickly for individual genes and in batch. It handles the management and integration of soybean genomics and multi-omics data along with gene function annotations, biological pathway and trait information. It has many useful tools including gene family search, multiple gene/metabolite analysis, motif analysis tool, protein 3D structure viewer and data download and upload capacity. It has a user-friendly web interface together with genome browser and pathway viewer, which displays data in an intuitive manner to the soybean researchers, breeders and consumers. The QTLs, SNP, GWAS and traits information is seamlessly integrated in our newly developed suite of tools for the In Silico Breeding Program. In addition, SoyKB now has capacity for incorporation of DNA methylation data and fast neutron mutation datasets. It is also linked seamlessly with P3DB for phosphorylation data. However, many protein-protein interactions (PPIs) have no tissue-contexts. The TissueNet database bridges this gap by associating experimentally-identified PPIs with human tissues that were shown to express both pair-mates. Users can select a protein and a tissue, and obtain a network view of the query protein and its tissue-associated PPIs. TissueNet v.2 is an updated version of the TissueNet database previously featured in NAR. It includes over 40 human tissues profiled via RNA-sequencing or protein-based assays. Users can select their preferred expression data source and interactively set the expression threshold for determining tissue-association. The output of TissueNet v.2 underlines qualitative and quantitative features of query proteins and their PPIs. The tissue-specificity view highlights tissue-specific and globally-expressed proteins, and the quantitative view highlights proteins that were differentially expressed in the selected tissue relative to all other tissues. Together, these views allow users to quickly assess the unique versus global functionality of query proteins. Thus, TissueNet v.2 offers an extensive, quantitative and user-friendly interface to study the roles of human proteins across tissues.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.sv2rm8","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.sv2rm8","homepage":"https://netbio.bgu.ac.il/tissuenet3/","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":2147,"registry":"database","record_type":"knowledgebase","name":"Manually Curated Database of Rice Proteins","abbreviation":"MCDRP","description":"The Manually Curated Database of Rice Proteins (MCDRP) is a manually-curated database based on published experimental data. By connecting with international initiatives such as the European Genome-phenome Archive (EGA) and the European Genomic Data Infrastructure (GDI), GHGA ensures that data is FAIRly stored, accessible, and interoperable across the globe.\n\nGHGA has set up a trusted, long-term repository, with plans to preserve data at every stage, from raw sequencing files to processed data and research results. Developed in phases, GHGA will continually add features that support secondary research, including secure processing environments, data statistics, and data analysis tools. In this way, GHGA provides a secure and evolving resource for researchers, accelerating scientific discoveries and the development of innovative healthcare solutions.\n\nRecognizing the sensitivity of human omics data, GHGA ensures strict data protection through robust security systems that maintain patient privacy and comply with ethical standards. Access to restricted datasets is not managed by GHGA, but by relevant Data Access Committees (DACs) appointed by the respective data controllers. DACs oversee requests based on established ethical and legal guidelines, while complying with the data subject’s consent. Resources with significant updates in the past year include BioProject (a biological project library), BioSample (a biological sample library), Genome Sequence Archive (GSA, a data repository for archiving raw sequence reads), Genome Warehouse (GWH, a centralized resource housing genome-scale data), Genome Variation Map (GVM, a public repository of genome variations), Science Wikis (a catalog of biological knowledge wikis for community annotations) and IC4R (Information Commons for Rice). Integrated analysis of scATAC+RNA data of mouse cerebral cortex in scMMO-atlas identified more cell subsets compared with unimodal omics data. Among these new cell subsets, there is an early astrocyte subset highly expressed Grm3, called Astro-Grm3. Furthermore, we identified Ex-L6-Tle4-Nrf1, a progenitor of Ex-L6-Tle4, indicating the statistical power provided by the big data in scMMO-atlas. With every full release a curated guide tree is provided that contains the latest taxonomy and nomenclature based on multiple references. The SILVA curation team is now part of Bergey’s Trustees and the Protist Reference Taxonomy Project UniEuk, which will further improve the quality of SILVA taxonomy. In order to cope with the dramatic expansion of bacterial and archaeal phyla, we have also teamed up with phylogenomic taxonomy efforts (i.e. Genome Taxonomy Database). SILVA is an ELIXIR Core Data Resource.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.5vtYGG","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.5vtYGG","homepage":"https://www.arb-silva.de","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":4937,"registry":"database","record_type":"knowledgebase","name":"JASPAR","abbreviation":null,"description":"JASPAR is a high-quality, open-access database that provides manually curated, non-redundant transcription factor (TF) binding profiles for six taxonomic groups (fungi, insecta, nematoda, plantae, urochordata, vertebrata). These profiles are stored as position frequency matrices (PFMs), which can be transformed into models to predict TF binding sites (TFBS) in DNA sequences. The database collects motifs both internally, analyzing sequences using a custom motif discovery pipeline, and externally from publications and other resources. TeloBase is supplemented by internal taxonomy utilizing popular on-line taxonomic resources that enables in-house data filtration and graphical visualisation of telomere DNA evolutionary dynamics in the form of heat tree plots. Its manual and other relevant data (raw results from NGS data analysis and Atlas of telomere sequences) can be accessed on https://www.ceitec.eu/chromatin-molecular-complexes/rg51/tab?tabId=125#TeloBase.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.c6edaa","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.c6edaa","homepage":"http://cfb.ceitec.muni.cz/telobase/","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":6240,"registry":"database","record_type":"repository","name":"Ocean Gene Atlas","abbreviation":"OGA","description":"Ocean Gene Atlas(OGA) is a webservice to explore the biogeography of marine genes based on sequence similarity with environmental genomics datasets. Moreover, because of the classification of an all-encompassing binding site of 85 residues it is possible to compare the interaction patterns of kinase-inhibitors to each other to, for example, identify crucial interactions determining kinase-inhibitor selectivity.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.mihb12","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.mihb12","homepage":"https://klifs.net","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":1714,"registry":"database","record_type":"repository","name":"IPD-IMGT/HLA Database","abbreviation":"IPD-IMGT/HLA","description":"The IPD-IMGT/HLA Database provides a specialist database for sequences of the human major histocompatibility complex (MHC) and includes the official sequences named by the WHO Nomenclature Committee For Factors of the HLA System. It aims to provide a reference database of carefully annotated 18S rRNA sequences using nine unique taxonomic fields (from domain to species). At present, it contains over 240,000 sequences. Although it focuses on protists, it also contains sequences from metazoa, fungi and plants as well a limited set of 16S sequences from plastids and bacteria. Several metadata fields are available for many sequences, including geo-localisation, whether it originates from a culture or a natural sample, and host type. The annotation of PR2 is performed by experts in each of the taxonomic groups. Nomenclatural experts will be available to check the validity, legitimacy and linguistic correctness of the proposed names in order to avoid nomenclatural error. Once public, the names are accessible through MycoBank, Index Fungorum, GBIF and other international biodiversity initiatives, where they will further be linked to other databases. Authors intending to publish nomenclatural novelties are encouraged to contribute.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.v8se8r","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.v8se8r","homepage":"http://www.mycobank.org","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":2158,"registry":"database","record_type":"knowledgebase","name":"The Improved Database Of Chimeric Transcripts and RNA-Seq Data","abbreviation":"ChiTaRS","description":"ChiTaRS 5.0 is the latest and most comprehensive chimeric transcript repository, with 111 582 annotated entries from eight species, including 23 167 known human cancer breakpoints. The database includes unique information correlating chimeric breakpoints with 3D chromatin contact maps, generated from public datasets of chromosome conformation capture techniques (Hi–C). In this update, we have added curated information on druggable fusion targets matched with chimeric breakpoints, which are applicable to precision medicine in cancers. The introduction of a new section that lists chimeric RNAs in various cell-lines is another salient feature. Finally, using text-mining techniques, novel chimeras in Alzheimer's disease, schizophrenia, dyslexia and other diseases were collected in ChiTaRS. Thus, this improved version is an extensive catalogue of chimeras from multiple species. It extends our understanding of the evolution of chimeric transcripts in eukaryotes and contributes to the analysis of 3D genome conformational changes and the functional role of chimeras in the etiopathogenesis of cancers and other complex diseases.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.gg60g3","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.gg60g3","homepage":"https://chitars.bioinfo.cnio.es/index.html","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":1715,"registry":"database","record_type":"repository","name":"IPD-NHKIR - Non-Human Killer-cell Immunoglobulin-like Receptors","abbreviation":"IPD-NHKIR","description":"The IPD-NHKIR database provides a centralised repository for non-human KIR (NHKIR) sequences. Killer-cell Immunoglobulin-like Receptors (KIR) have been shown to be highly polymorphic at the allelic and haplotypic level. KIRs are members of the immunoglobulin superfamily (IgSF) formerly called Killer-cell Inhibitory Receptors. They are composed of two or three Ig-domains, a transmembrane region and cytoplasmic tail which can in turn be short (activatory) or long (inhibitory). Ciliates, like the Last Eukaryotic Common Ancestor, have the axonemal structure in the form of vibrating cilia that assure locomotion and food capture and are essential for pairing of paramecia of opposite mating types during conjugation (sexual reproduction). Like multicellular eukaryotes, ciliates separate germinal and somatic functions, harbouring two different kinds of nuclei. A diploid germinal micronucleus transmits the genetic information to the next sexual generation, while a polyploid somatic macronucleus is responsible for gene expression. Reproducible DNA elimination, which occurs in many animal lineages, takes place in ciliates during sexual reproduction, when a new somatic macronucleus develops from a copy of the germline micronucleus.\nThe somatic genomes of numerous Paramecium species are being sequenced and annotated. ParameciumDB integrates the genomes as they become available, to provide resources for functional and comparative genomics to the community. ParameciumDB interfaces and tools have been redesigned. (Grant No. 1U24CA224370-01). The goal of KMC is to develop a comprehensive, integrated knowledge-base for the Druggable Genome (DG) to illuminate the uncharacterized and/or poorly annotated portion of the DG, focusing on three of the most commonly drug-targeted protein families: G-protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs) ; Ion channels (ICs) and Kinases.\n","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.52d6ae","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.52d6ae","homepage":"https://pharos.nih.gov/","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":2598,"registry":"database","record_type":"knowledgebase","name":"SalmoBase","abbreviation":"SalmoBase","description":"SalmoBase is an integrated molecular resource for Salmonid species which includes visualizations and analytic tools. A public genome assembly is available and ordered into 29 chromosome files. SalmoBase includes a GBrowse interface for exploring the chromosome sequence as well as BLAST functionality and links to expression levels, gene and SNP information. Using public RNAseq data 37,000 high confidence protein coding genes have been identified, wherein almost half show splice variants.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.97kZl6","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.97kZl6","homepage":"http://salmobase.org","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":5916,"registry":"database","record_type":"knowledgebase","name":"Glittr.org","abbreviation":null,"description":"Glittr.org is a curated database of educational material for the computational life sciences.\n\nAll material is:\n- In a GitHub or GitLab repository\n- Free to use\n- Written in markdown or similar","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.aa243c","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.aa243c","homepage":"https://glittr.org","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":1975,"registry":"database","record_type":"repository","name":"Gene Expression Omnibus","abbreviation":"GEO","description":"The Gene Expression Omnibus (GEO) is a public repository that archives and freely distributes microarray, next-generation sequencing, and other forms of high-throughput functional genomic data submitted by the scientific community. Accepts next generation sequence data that examine quantitative gene expression, gene regulation, epigenomics or other aspects of functional genomics using methods such as RNA-seq, miRNA-seq, ChIP-seq, RIP-seq, HiC-seq, methyl-seq, etc. GEO will process all components of your study, including the samples, project description, processed data files, and will submit the raw data files to the Sequence Read Archive (SRA) on the researchers behalf. In addition to data storage, a collection of web-based interfaces and applications are available to help users query and download the studies and gene expression patterns stored in GEO.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.5hc8vt","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.5hc8vt","homepage":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/geo/","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":2953,"registry":"database","record_type":"knowledgebase","name":"Silkworm Pathogen Database","abbreviation":"SilkPathDB","description":"Silkworm Pathogen Database (SilkPathDB) is a comprehensive resource for studying on pathogens of silkworm, including microsporidia, fungi, bacteria and virus. SilkPathDB provides access to not only genomic data including functional annotation of genes and gene products, but also extensive biological information for gene expression data and corresponding researches. SilkPathDB will be help with researches on pathogens of silkworm as well as other Lepidoptera insects.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.801eea","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.801eea","homepage":"https://silkpathdb.swu.edu.cn/","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":2110,"registry":"database","record_type":"knowledgebase","name":"UNITE","abbreviation":null,"description":"UNITE is a database and sequence management environment centered on the eukaryotic nuclear ribosomal ITS region. All eukaryotic ITS sequences from the International Nucleotide Sequence Database Collaboration are clustered to approximately the species level (distance between species in steps of 0.5%), and all such species hypotheses are given a DOI to facilitate unambiguous scientific communication and data assembly. The user can interact with these species hypotheses (e.g., SH1566366.08FU) in various ways, and third-party sequence and metadata annotation are supported. Registration is needed to access the more powerful features of the accompanying PlutoF workbench. A range of search and query options are available, and precompiled reference datasets for local sequence similarity searches and HTS pipelines are available for download. Owing to the mycological roots of UNITE, two versions of the release files are available: all eukaryotes (including fungi), and fungi only. UNITE is a community effort that relies on the scientific expertise of its users – please consider contributing by, e.g., annotating sequences of your taxa of expertise.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.cnwx8c","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.cnwx8c","homepage":"https://unite.ut.ee/index.php","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":1934,"registry":"database","record_type":"knowledgebase_and_repository","name":"SoyBase","abbreviation":"SoyBase","description":"SoyBase, the USDA-ARS soybean genetic database, is a comprehensive repository for professionally curated genetics, genomics and related data resources for soybean. SoyBase contains genetic, physical and genomic sequence maps integrated with qualitative and quantitative traits. The quantitative trait loci (QTL) represent more than 18 years of QTL mapping of more than 90 unique traits. SoyBase also contains the well-annotated 'Williams 82' genomic sequence and associated data mining tools. The genetic and sequence views of the soybean chromosomes and the extensive data on traits and phenotypes are extensively interlinked. This allows entry to the database using almost any kind of available information, such as genetic map symbols, soybean gene names or phenotypic traits. SoyBase is the repository for controlled vocabularies for soybean growth, development and trait terms, which are also linked to the more general plant ontologies.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.z4agsr","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.z4agsr","homepage":"https://soybase.org/","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":1977,"registry":"database","record_type":"knowledgebase","name":"NCBI HomoloGene Database","abbreviation":"HomoloGene","description":"HomoloGene is an automated system for the detection of homologs among the annotated genes of several completely sequenced eukaryotic genomes. HomoloGene takes protein sequences from differing species and compares them to one another (using blastp) and matches are placed into groups, using a tree built from sequence similarity to guide the process, where closer related organisms are matched up first, and then further organisms are added as the tree is traversed toward the root. The protein alignments are mapped back to their corresponding DNA sequences, where distance metrics can be calculated (e.g. molecular distance, Ka/Ks ratio). Sequences are matched using synteny when applicable. Remaining sequences are matched up by using an algorithm for maximizing the score globally, rather than locally, in a bipartite matching. Cutoffs on bits per position and Ks values are set to prevent unlikely \"orthologs\" from being grouped together. These cutoffs are calculated based on the respective score distribution for the given groups of organisms. Paralogs are identified by finding sequences that are closer within species than other species.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.mzc066","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.mzc066","homepage":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/homologene","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":1576,"registry":"database","record_type":"repository","name":"EcoliWiki","abbreviation":null,"description":"EcoliWiki is a community-based resource for the annotation of all non-pathogenic E. coli, its phages, plasmids, and mobile genetic elements. Users can contribute data to this wiki-style repository.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.tx95wa","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.tx95wa","homepage":"https://ecoliwiki.org/colipedia/index.php/Welcome_to_EcoliWiki","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":1598,"registry":"database","record_type":"knowledgebase_and_repository","name":"JGI Integrated Microbial Genomes And Microbiomes","abbreviation":"JGI IMG/M","description":"The Integrated Microbial Genomes (IMG/M) aims to support the annotation, analysis and distribution of microbial genome and microbiome datasets sequenced at DOE's Joint Genome Institute (JGI). The IMG data warehouse integrates genome and metagenome datasets provided by IMG users with a set of publicly available genome and metagenome datasets. IMG/M is also open to scientists worldwide for the annotation, analysis, and distribution of their own genome and microbiome datasets, as long as they agree with the IMG/M data release policy and follow the metadata requirements for integrating data into IMG/M.","doi":"10.25504/FAIRsharing.ae956n","url":"https://doi.org/10.25504/FAIRsharing.ae956n","homepage":"http://img.jgi.doe.gov/cgi-bin/m/main.cgi","countries":[],"disciplines":[],"domains":[],"taxonomies":[],"user_defined_tags":[],"legacy_ids":[],"created_at":"","updated_at":""},{"id":2291,"registry":"database","record_type":"repository","name":"iDog","abbreviation":"iDog","description":"iDog is an integrated resource for domestic dog (Canis lupus familiaris) and wild canids that provides the worldwide dog research community a variety of data services. APA has thus been implicated in the regulation of cell proliferation, differentiation and disease development. PolyASite is a portal to the curated set of poly(A) sites inferred from publicly available 3’ end sequencing datasets. It allows efficient finding, exploration and export of poly(A) sites in several organisms. In constructing our poly(A) site atlas, we applied uniform quality measures, including the flagging of putative internal priming sites, to input datasets originating from different 3’ end sequencing techniques, cell types or tissues. Through integrated processing of all data, we identified and clustered sites that are closely spaced and share polyadenylation signals, as these are likely the result of stochastic variations in processing. For each cluster, we identified the representative - most frequently processed - site and estimated the relative use in the transcriptome across all samples. While other existing alternative polyadenylation databases rely on a single experimental method, or on poly(A) site estimation from RNA-seq data, PolyASite is built on data from several 3’ end sequencing techniques. Heritable features - both genotypes (e.g., DNA sequences) and phenotypes (e.g., anatomy, behavior, physiology) are stored as part of the Tree of Life project. While the genomic part of this work is archived at the National Center for Biotechnology Information (NCBI), MorphoBank is part of the infrastructure for storing and sharing phenotype data, including information on anatomy, physiology, behavior and other features of species. One can think of MorphoBank as two databases in one: one that permits researchers to upload images and affiliate data with those images (labels, species names, etc.) and a second database that allows researchers to upload morphological data and affiliate it with phylogenetic matrices. When a paper associated with the project is published, the research team can make their data permanently available for view on MorphoBank where it is now archived. MINRbase provides interactive access to the profiles of 130 238 expressed rRFs arising from the four human nuclear rRNAs (18S, 5.8S, 28S, 5S), two mitochondrial rRNAs (12S, 16S) or four spacers of 45S pre-rRNA. We compiled these profiles by analyzing 11 632 datasets, including the GEUVADIS and The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) repositories. MINRbase offers a user-friendly interface that lets researchers issue complex queries based on one or more criteria, such as parental rRNA identity, nucleotide sequence, rRF minimum abundance and metadata keywords (e.g. tissue type, disease). A 'summary' page for each rRF provides a granular breakdown of its expression by tissue type, disease, sex, ancestry and other variables; it also allows users to create publication-ready plots at the click of a button. MINRbase has already allowed us to generate support for three novel observations: the internal spacers of 45S are prolific producers of abundant rRFs; many abundant rRFs straddle the known boundaries of rRNAs; rRF production is regimented and depends on 'personal attributes' (sex, ancestry) and 'context' (tissue type, tissue state, disease).
